---
title: "Ch 13 — Introduction to Graphs"
subtitle: "Two number lines crossed — a place for every pair of numbers"
lesson-id: "cbse8.introduction-to-graphs"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 13
ncert-chapter-title: "Introduction to Graphs"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.data-and-average", "t1.number-line"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.coordinate-geometry", "cbse9.linear-equations-two-var", "cbse10.coordinate-geometry"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "databases", "game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you read a thermometer or a ruler? If not, revisit
   **[The Number Line](../../tier-1-foundations/02-number-line/index.qmd)**.
2. Have you ever made a bar chart by hand? If not, revisit
   **[Data and Average](../../tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)**.
3. Do you know what *positive* and *negative* mean on a number line?
4. Can you describe a place in your school using "two streets across,
   then one street up"?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Find a sheet of squared graph paper, or imagine one. Draw a horizontal
line across the middle and a vertical line down the middle. Where they
cross, write $0$. To the right of the cross, mark $1, 2, 3, \ldots$.
Going up, mark $1, 2, 3, \ldots$.

Pick a place on the paper. *How* would you tell a friend where it is,
just using two numbers?

The slider below shows the same idea.

```{ojs}
viewof px = Inputs.range([0, 10], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "Across (x)"})
viewof py = Inputs.range([0, 10], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "Up (y)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const k = 30;
  const margin = 25;
  const cells = [];
  for (let i = 0; i <= 10; i++) {
    cells.push(`<line x1="${margin+i*k}" y1="${margin}" x2="${margin+i*k}" y2="${margin+10*k}" stroke="#eee"/>`);
    cells.push(`<line x1="${margin}" y1="${margin+i*k}" x2="${margin+10*k}" y2="${margin+i*k}" stroke="#eee"/>`);
  }
  cells.push(`<line x1="${margin}" y1="${margin}" x2="${margin}" y2="${margin+10*k}" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>`);
  cells.push(`<line x1="${margin}" y1="${margin+10*k}" x2="${margin+10*k}" y2="${margin+10*k}" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>`);
  const cx = margin + px*k;
  const cy = margin + (10-py)*k;
  cells.push(`<line x1="${margin}" y1="${cy}" x2="${cx}" y2="${cy}" stroke="#1d4ed8" stroke-dasharray="3,3"/>`);
  cells.push(`<line x1="${cx}" y1="${margin+10*k}" x2="${cx}" y2="${cy}" stroke="#1d4ed8" stroke-dasharray="3,3"/>`);
  cells.push(`<circle cx="${cx}" cy="${cy}" r="6" fill="#dc2626"/>`);
  cells.push(`<text x="${cx+10}" y="${cy-6}" font-size="13" font-family="system-ui">(${px}, ${py})</text>`);
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;">
    <svg width="${margin*2 + 10*k}" height="${margin*2 + 10*k}" style="background:#fafafa;">
      ${cells.join("")}
    </svg>
  </div>`;
}
```

Slide the two numbers. The red dot moves. Two numbers determine a
position. The first says *across*; the second says *up*. They are not
interchangeable.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. If you swap the two numbers — point $(4, 3)$ becomes $(3, 4)$ — do
   you arrive at the same place?
2. The point $(0, 5)$ sits *on* the up-line. The point $(5, 0)$ sits
   *on* the across-line. The point $(0, 0)$ sits where?
3. What do you call the point a temperature graph touches when the
   temperature is $0\,°$C?
4. Can you imagine a chart where every dot is the *same* height?
   Could you describe that chart in just one sentence?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

A horizontal line is "all dots have the same up-value". A vertical
line is "all dots have the same across-value". Lines that climb
left-to-right are the next step — and they encode the relationship
$y = mx + b$. The chapter starts with bar charts and ends with
straight-line graphs of linear equations.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Plot a point

On graph paper (or in your head), plot the point $(3, 4)$. Then plot
$(4, 3)$. Are they the same point?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First number = how far right; second number = how far up.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(3, 4)$ is $3$ right and $4$ up. $(4, 3)$ is $4$ right and $3$ up.
**They are different points.** Order matters.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Read a bar chart

A bar chart of fruit sales (in kg) shows: apple $30$, banana $50$,
orange $20$, mango $40$. (i) Which fruit sold most? (ii) Which sold
least? (iii) Total kg sold?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find the tallest and shortest bars; sum the heights.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** Banana ($50$ kg).
**(ii)** Orange ($20$ kg).
**(iii)** Total $= 30 + 50 + 20 + 40 = 140$ kg.
</details>

### Problem 3 — A linear graph

Plot the points $(0, 1), (1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 7)$ on graph paper. What
shape do they form? Can you spot a rule connecting $x$ and $y$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Check: when $x$ goes up by $1$, what happens to $y$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$x$ goes up by $1$, $y$ goes up by $2$. So $y = 2x + ?$. Plug in $x =
0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The four points lie on a **straight line**. Each unit right takes you
$2$ units up. At $x = 0$, $y = 1$. So $y = 2x + 1$.

(All four points satisfy $y = 2x + 1$. Verify: $2 \cdot 3 + 1 = 7$. ✓)
</details>

### Problem 4 — Read a linear graph

A graph shows distance against time for a car: at $t = 0$ hour the
distance is $0$ km; at $t = 2$ the distance is $120$ km; at $t = 4$
it is $240$ km. (i) What is the speed? (ii) How far at $t = 7$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Speed = distance change / time change. (Constant speed → straight line
through origin.)
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** Speed $= 120 / 2 = 60$ km/h.

**(ii)** Distance $= 60 \times 7 = 420$ km.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Fill in the table

If $y = 3x - 1$, complete the table and plot the points.

| $x$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $y$ | ? | ? | ? | ? |

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| $x$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $y$ | $-1$ | $2$ | $5$ | $8$ |

When plotted, all four points lie on a straight line. They all satisfy
$y = 3x - 1$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Pie vs bar vs line

You want to display: (i) percentage marks of one student in different
subjects, (ii) population of a town from year 1980 to 2020, (iii) the
share of expenses by category in a household budget. For each, which
of *bar*, *line*, *pie* is the most natural choice?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Categories vs over-time vs share-of-whole.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(i) bar chart (categories: subjects). (ii) line graph (continuous time
trend). (iii) pie chart (parts of a whole).
</details>

You have just plotted points, read straight-line graphs, computed
slope from two readings, and matched chart-type to data type. That is
the chapter.

::: {.fun-fact}
The idea of pinning a point in the plane with two numbers was published
in 1637 by **René Descartes** as an appendix — *La Géométrie* — to his
philosophical *Discours de la méthode*. Legend (probably embellished)
says Descartes was lying ill in bed watching a fly crawl across the
ceiling, and realised its position was perfectly fixed by its distance
from two adjacent walls. Whatever the truth, his appendix married
algebra to geometry: every equation became a curve, every curve an
equation. The Cartesian plane on your graph paper is named for him.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The [coordinate plane](/glossary.qmd#coordinate-plane) is the place where
**algebra and geometry meet**. An equation like $y = 2x + 1$ is a *rule*;
its graph is a *picture*; the picture is a straight line precisely because
the rule is linear. The [slope](/glossary.qmd#slope) is the rule's "pace
of change", and the [y-intercept](/glossary.qmd#y-intercept) is its
"starting point". Two numbers, one line.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **Cartesian plane** is two number lines crossed at right angles
at the **origin** $(0, 0)$. The horizontal line is the **$x$-axis**;
the vertical line is the **$y$-axis**. Every point is named by an
**ordered pair** $(x, y)$ — first $x$ across, then $y$ up.

A **linear graph** is a straight line. The graph of $y = mx + c$ is a
straight line; $m$ is the **slope** (how much $y$ changes per unit of
$x$) and $c$ is the **$y$-intercept** (the $y$-value at $x = 0$).

**Common graph types:**
- *Bar chart* — separate categories.
- *Histogram* — continuous numerical bins (no gaps).
- *Pie chart* — share of a whole.
- *Line graph* — change over a continuous variable, especially time.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Across is x, up is y; the cross is the origin's home.*
> *Two numbers, in order — one place; reverse them, you've roamed.*
> *Steps right by one, jumps up by m — slope, and the line is born:*
> *$y = m x + c$ — c is where it cuts the upright at noon.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> The cost of $1$ kg of a vegetable is $\text{₹}40$. (i) Make a table
> showing cost for $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ kg. (ii) Draw a graph of cost (on
> $y$-axis) against weight (on $x$-axis). (iii) Use the graph to find
> the cost of $3.5$ kg.

Competent solver's path:

**(i)**

| Weight (kg) | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Cost (₹) | $40$ | $80$ | $120$ | $160$ | $200$ |

**(ii)** All five points lie on the line $y = 40 x$, passing through
the origin.

**(iii)** At $x = 3.5$, $y = 40 \cdot 3.5 = \text{₹}140$. Reading the
graph at $3.5$ kg gives the same answer.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Plot the points $A(2, 5), B(0, 4), C(-1, 3), D(3, 0)$ and name
the axes each lies on, if any.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$y$-axis: $x = 0$. $x$-axis: $y = 0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A$ is in the interior. $B$ lies on the $y$-axis. $C$ is in the
interior (this lesson concentrates on the first quadrant — negative
coordinates appear in *Class 9*). $D$ lies on the $x$-axis.
</details>

**W2.** From the table $y = 2x$ for $x \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$, plot
the points. Are they collinear?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(0,0), (1,2), (2,4), (3,6), (4,8)$ — all on the line $y = 2x$. Yes,
collinear.
</details>

**W3.** A line passes through $(0, 5)$ and $(2, 9)$. Find its slope.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Slope = (change in $y$) / (change in $x$).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{9 - 5}{2 - 0} = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A car uses petrol at $\text{₹}100$ per litre. Make a table
showing cost for $1$ to $5$ litres, and draw the graph.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cost $= 100 \times $ litres.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Litres | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Cost (₹) | $100$ | $200$ | $300$ | $400$ | $500$ |

The graph is a straight line through the origin with slope $100$.
</details>

**P2.** A bar chart of monthly savings (in $\text{₹}$) of a family for
$5$ months: Jan $2000$, Feb $1500$, Mar $2500$, Apr $1800$, May $3000$.
Find the (i) total saved, (ii) average saving per month.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** Total $= 2000 + 1500 + 2500 + 1800 + 3000 = \text{₹}10800$.

**(ii)** Average $= 10800 / 5 = \text{₹}2160$.
</details>

**P3.** Plot $A(1, 1), B(1, 4), C(5, 4), D(5, 1)$. What shape is
$ABCD$? Find its area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The two pairs of opposite sides are vertical and horizontal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$ABCD$ is a **rectangle** (sides $4$ and $3$ at right angles).
Width $|AD| = 5 - 1 = 4$. Height $|AB| = 4 - 1 = 3$.
Area $= 4 \times 3 = 12$ square units.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** The graph of $y = mx$ passes through $(4, 12)$. Find $m$, and
the value of $y$ when $x = 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute the point. $12 = 4m$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$m = 3$. So $y = 3x$. At $x = 9$: $y = 27$.
</details>

**S2.** A water tank holds $200$ litres. A tap fills it at $5$ litres
per minute. (i) Make a table showing water in the tank at $t = 0, 5,
10, 15, 20$ minutes. (ii) Plot the line graph. (iii) After how many
minutes will the tank be full?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume $V = 5t$ until full.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| $t$ (min) | $0$ | $5$ | $10$ | $15$ | $20$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $V$ (L) | $0$ | $25$ | $50$ | $75$ | $100$ |

Line through origin, slope $5$.

Tank full when $V = 200 \Rightarrow t = 200 / 5 = 40$ minutes.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The point $(0, 0)$ is called the
(a) origin (b) abscissa (c) ordinate (d) coordinate.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Answer: (a) origin.**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Plot the points $(2, 3)$ and $(2, 7)$. Find
the distance between them.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The two points share an $x$-coordinate. The line is vertical.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Distance $= |7 - 3| = 4$ units.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Draw the graph of $y = 2x + 3$ for
$x = -1, 0, 1, 2$. Use the graph to find $y$ when $x = 1.5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute the four $y$-values, plot, then read between the third and
fourth points.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| $x$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $y$ | $1$ | $3$ | $5$ | $7$ |

All four points lie on the same straight line.

By computation: $y = 2(1.5) + 3 = 6$.
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** A factory's monthly production for the
first $6$ months of $2024$ (in thousand units) was: Jan $20$, Feb
$24$, Mar $32$, Apr $30$, May $40$, Jun $44$. (i) Draw a line graph.
(ii) Find the total production for the half-year. (iii) In which
month was the increase from the previous month largest?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Plot months on $x$-axis and production on $y$-axis. Compute differences
between consecutive months.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

**(i)** Plot the six points $(1, 20), (2, 24), (3, 32), (4, 30), (5,
40), (6, 44)$ and join with line segments.

**(ii)** Total $= 20 + 24 + 32 + 30 + 40 + 44 = 190$ thousand units
$= 1{,}90{,}000$ units.

**(iii)** Differences: Feb $+4$, Mar $+8$, Apr $-2$, May $+10$, Jun
$+4$. **Largest jump: May (+10)**.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 13.4 (line graph,
total/average/biggest-change) is a near-twin of the 4-mark item.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2018 Set 1, Q17 ("plot $y = 2x + 1$
and find $y$ at a non-integer $x$") matches the 3-mark item closely.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$(3, 4)$ is the same as $(4, 3)$."** No. The first number is
   *across* (x); the second is *up* (y). Order matters absolutely.
2. **"A bar chart and a histogram are the same."** Bar charts have
   gaps between bars (separate categories). Histograms have no gaps
   (continuous bins). The kind of data on the $x$-axis decides which.
3. **"A line graph means values are continuous between data
   points."** Joining data points with line segments is a *visual aid*,
   not always a claim that the data really took those in-between
   values. (E.g., monthly sales — there is no "1.5 March".)
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember these:

1. The [coordinate plane](/glossary.qmd#coordinate-plane) names every
   point with an *ordered* pair $(x, y)$ — across first, up second.
   Swap the order and you land somewhere else.
2. A linear rule $y = mx + c$ has a straight-line graph. The
   [slope](/glossary.qmd#slope) $m$ tells you the rise per step right;
   the [y-intercept](/glossary.qmd#y-intercept) $c$ tells you where the
   line crosses the vertical axis.
3. Different data want different charts: **bar** for categories,
   **histogram** for continuous bins, **pie** for shares of a whole,
   **line** for change over time.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you, with no help, plot four points from a table and
   confirm whether they are collinear?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the
   7 *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Given a real-world question (e.g., "show this household's monthly
   spending"), can you pick the right chart type without thinking?

- **3 yes:** congratulations — you have completed the **Class 8 NCERT
  curriculum** in this track. Move on to **[Class 9 — Number Systems](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/01-number-systems/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Stretch S2* and the 4-mark board item, then move
  on.
- **0–1 yes:** redo *Discover Problem 3* — the leap from a table of
  numbers to a straight-line graph is the keystone of this chapter.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[The Number Line](../../tier-1-foundations/02-number-line/index.qmd)** — placing a single number on a line.
- **[Data and Average](../../tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)** — bar charts, mean, mode.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 9 — Coordinate Geometry](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/03-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)** — adds the four quadrants and signed coordinates.
- **[Class 9 — Linear Equations in Two Variables](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/04-linear-equations-two-vars/index.qmd)** — a straight line *is* the solution set of a two-variable linear equation.
- **[Class 10 — Coordinate Geometry](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)** — distance and section formula.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 13 — Introduction to Graphs** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 13.1–13.3: bar graphs,
  histograms, pie charts, line graphs, and the Cartesian plane in the
  first quadrant.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t2.coordinate-plane`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd)
  treats the coordinate plane at the synthesis level (all four
  quadrants); this chapter focuses on the first quadrant and on
  reading and producing data graphs.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every plot you have ever seen produced by a machine
learning library — loss curves, precision-recall, confusion matrices
— is built on the Cartesian plane and the line-graph idea you just
formalised.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Every query result returned to a dashboard is a row
of $(x, y)$ pairs, awaiting plotting. The first chart any analyst
draws on new data is the line graph from this chapter.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every position in a 2D game is an ordered pair
$(x, y)$ on a screen-sized Cartesian plane. The same plotting rules
move characters, fire bullets, and decide which sprite is on top.
:::
